data.title: Alphabet: Why Strong Money Likes It At 27x P/E
data.description:
Alphabet stock: super-investors love it at $313

I last covered Alphabet Inc. (GOOG, GOOGL) on Feb 23 with an article titled "Alphabet: SpaceX IPO, 100-Year Bond, And My New Understanding Of Its Moat.” As stated in the title, the article was triggered by Google’s SpaceX (SPACE) holdings and also new bond issuance, and it rated the stock as Buy. Since then, there have been a few new developments worth noting. In the rest of this article, I will focus on two of them: the latest changes in professional fund managers’ holdings and also the latest AI traffic data. These changes, when combined, suggest the stock’s growth potential is discounted, thus offering a favorable return/risk ratio and leading to a reiteration of my Buy rating.

Let me start with the changes of the so-called super-investors’ GOOG holdings. In my experiences, I benefited more from observing these investors’ transactions than following the overall market. The reasons are simply twofold in my mind. For these investors, A) their decisions are less biased by herd thinking, and B) their investment timeframe tends to be longer. With this background, the following screenshot shows the latest holdings of GOOG stock among super-investors tracked by DataRoma.

As seen, GOOG currently ranks top 2 in terms of ownership and top 3 in terms concentration (i.e., percentage allocation in their portfolios). Overall, a total of 29 super-investors has a GOOG position, the stock makes up more than 2.2% of their accounts, and their current hold price is ~$313. The current market price for GOOG is about $296 as of this writing, which is quite close to their holding price (about 5.5% lower) and makes their decision of particular interest.

Next, I will explain why these investors see a good deal at $313 and the return/risk curve is even more favorable at the current price of $296.

GOOG stock: growth outlook and return potential

Before going into any further details, let’s me start with a broader picture. The figure below shows my back-of-envelope estimates for GOOG’s return potential under various combinations of growth and valuation multiples. These estimates were made for a holding period of 5 years, but I believe the essence remains the safe for other holding periods if they are sufficiently long to filter out short-term noises.

I am especially interested in the total ROI potential (return on investment) for 3 P/E ratios. These ratios are 25.5x, 27.0x, and 28.2x. They are shown by the blue, green, and red curves in the chart, respectively. I am particularly interested in these 3 cases because they are GOOG’s current FWD P/E in the open market, the P/E ratio at the average holding price of $313 among super-investors, and also the current P/E ratio of the S&P 500 (SP500). Given GOOG’s scale and positioning among the SP500 (more on this in a minute), I see large odds for an exit P/E within the range of these 3 ratios.

Now, for its growth outlook, consensus estimates point to a compounded annual growth rate (CAGR) of 17.3% for the next 6 years as shown in the next chart. My personal view is that the consensus estimates are too aggressive. Based on my analysis of its ROCE (return on capital employed, which averages about 63% in recent years) and its long-term reinvestment rates (RR), my own growth projection is around 13% per annum on average. It is true that the RR is extremely high in recent years and will remain so in FY 2026 too due to the surges to AI-related capex projects. However, I expect the RR to revert to an average of 20% in the longer term, resulting in an organic growth rate of 13% on average (which equals the product of 63% ROCE and 20% RR).

With the above variances of exit P/E and growth rates anticipated above, the purple box in the figure illustrates the range of potential ROI. To wit, the upper-right corner of the purple box illustrates the best-case scenario in my view, featuring a combination of high growth (17.3% CAGR as consensus projects) and a high exit P/E ratio of 28.2x. In this case, the stock could deliver a total return of about 135% in 5 years. Similarly, the lower-left corner of the purple box illustrates the worst-case scenario (that is, in relative terms), featuring a combination of lower growth (13% CAGR per my estimate) and a lower P/E ratio of 25.5x. In this case, the stock could still deliver a total return of about 70% in 5 years.

GOOG stock: latest AI traffic data

In either case, I see a quite favorable return/risk curve under current condition. Given the many catalysts afoot, I indeed see good odds for double-digit annual EPS growth rates in the years to come. My recent articles have provided in-depth analyses on some of these catalyst ranging from its capital allocation flexibility to its in-house AI chips.

Today, I wanted to bring your attention to the momentum of its Gemini AI models. Since my last article, several digital analytic services (such as Similarweb, Statcounter, etc.) have provided updates to web traffic data, in particular traffic data surrounding AI models. After reviewing these updates, my overall impression is twofold. First, Google's search still dominates the global market share. The latest data showed an increase of 14 basis points month-over-month to a share of 90% in February 2026. Second, GOOG’s Gemeni model began to gain momentum and demonstrate faster growth compared to ChatGPT (see details quoted from the following report).

According to Similarweb data, all major AI platforms saw varying degrees of month-over-month growth in global average daily web visits during February. Google led significantly with 290 million daily visits, up 6% year-over-year and 4% month-over-month. ChatGPT recorded 197 million daily visits, up 42% year-over-year and 7% month-over-month. Gemini had 79 million daily visits, surging 667% year-over-year and increasing 17% month-over-month.

Other risks and final thoughts

With these developments, I see a powerful and positive interplay among GOOG’s existing business segments and new segments. In the case of search and AI, GOOG enjoys a virtuous cycle where each strengthens the other’s utility and commercial reach. GOOG’s dominance in the search space provides the real-time ground truth and a vast amount of data, while the Gemini AI tool provides the reasoning and synthesis. In terms of financials, I anticipate existing segments such as search keep generating robust cash flow to fund the continued investments in new segments.

Speaking of the latest AI traffic data, the growth of Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude model was particularly notable (see the following report for more details) and was equally impressive to the Gemini’s growth, as quoted above.

Claude reached 11 million global daily visits, a 308% year-over-year increase and a substantial 63% month-over-month jump. This trend continued in the US market: Claude's US daily visits reached 3.3 million, up 353% year-over-year and 67% month-over-month.

The relationship between Google and Claude a nuanced coopetition in my view, a blend of deep financial partnership and aggressive product rivalry. Google has a sizable stake in Anthropic, and this stake acts as a hedge for GOOG shareholders. If Anthropic’s AI model wins the market, Google shareholder can profit through the stake. Anthropic is also major customer of Google Cloud under the current arrangement, so its success could also drive revenue for Google’s cloud and hardware divisions. However, the rise of Claude could also cause GOOG to lose pentanal clients especially high-value clients. My own experience is that Claude is far better in coding and mathematical tasks, while Gemini shines in more general tasks. As such, I see a potential for high-value customers (engineers, analysts, and researchers) to adapt to Claude in a way similar to how these groups favor MatLab over more general coding/analysis software.

To wrap up, this GOOG revisit was mainly triggered by two new developments since my last writing: the latest disclosures of super-investors’ holdings and the latest AI traffic data. Recent volatility in the broader market has caused GOOG share price to fall below these investors’ average holding price by about 5%. My estimates show a favorable ROI potential at their holding P/E, which becomes even more favorable at the current share prices. The return/risk curve is further skewed by the latest market share data for GOOG’s search and AI tools. With these considerations, I reiterate my Buy rating.

data.title: Adobe: Stop The Wishful Thinking And Face The Reality
data.description:
Adobe: One Way Street (Downwards)

The mood among Adobe Inc. (ADBE) investors right now must be highly depressing.

When we consider the long-term chart showing the "one-way street" in its price action, I don't think any of us would disagree.

What worries me more is that the decline happened since Q3 of 2024, suggesting a multi-year digestion of its premium multiple hasn't been adequately addressed.

For now, a near-term bottom appears to be more likely than not. But, should investors contemplate returning right now?

Adobe: Big AI Spenders Are Looking Elsewhere

More intense competitive challenges have already worried Adobe investors. We already know that Figma (FIG) and Canva are proving more than being capable rivals.

Right now, they are clearly also attracting higher AI spending among larger enterprise customers at the expense of Adobe.

From the above table, we can see why, as ADBE notched a -15% YoY change metric.

Of course, there's no need to freak out all of a sudden right now. But, these are justified concerns and something that we cannot simply dismiss (no longer).

Still, it's helpful to note that Adobe also posted more than $26 billion in ARR recently. So, while the forthcoming threats are real, they don't pose an imminent disruption, yet.

Moreover, while the active user base for Adobe’s smaller peers is growing quickly, Canva ($4B annualized revenue as of the end of 2025) and Figma (>$1 billion for FY2025), this revenue is nowhere close to threatening it yet, right?

Hence, even as the market assumes higher implied growth multiples for Canva and Figma, we also have to recognize that all of them face similar disruption risks from pure play AI LLMs.

But for us to suggest that Adobe is surely doomed is too big a call to make, because I haven’t seen that thesis playing out in its core P&L performances just yet.

Still, I suggested in my previous ADBE writeup, the problems could be worse than we had thought.

And due to these simmering uncertainties, the market could place it under the microscope for an extended duration to see how this AI disruption thesis plays out.

Furthermore, the lackluster performance as observed in the large AI spenders table may continue to put persistent downward pressure on the stock.

That's a handful for investors to handle because Adobe’s business model is obviously coming under pressure from its most direct (but smaller) rivals.

And now, we have to manage transition issues with management, as we know that CEO Shantanu Narayen has already decided to step down once they have managed to identify his successor.

I don't think there's a need for us to speculate further on why Narayen decided to hand over his long-held role in the firm, as the ongoing SaaS disruptive themes might benefit from new leadership perspectives.

ADBE: Value Stock Or Value Trap?

The market’s “judgment” on Adobe’s AI transformation plans under Narayen's leadership so far has been a resounding “poor,” as its implied growth premium has been wiped out.

Trading at just over 10x forward earnings, one might now think about ADBE as a deep value play or a value trap.

Compared to the SaaS median multiple (IGV) of just under 19x, the disconnect is glaring, and the loss of confidence astounding, if you know what I mean.

And when we consider that ADBE’s valuation has dropped to around the $100 billion mark, I’m not sure whether I could even term it as a software behemoth these days.

Is the market expecting the company’s position within the creative software suite to finally be untangled in the Generative AI era?

Adobe's P&L Still Look Pretty Solid

Which side you are standing on will really depend on whether you think Adobe’s much prized margins over the software industry still hold true.

Or could the company now be compelled to lower its high margins to compete even more aggressively against peers and AI companies that are going after its businesses?

Can Adobe lower its margins without suffering another investor exodus?

On a GAAP basis, it posted almost 90% gross margins. Operating margins were also 37% last year. And when I compare that to the 5-year average of 36%, I hardly see stress sensors blaring out loud, right?

It’s as if the market has taken on a totally different personality from the person preparing the financial statements for Adobe.

In other words, Adobe’s fundamental results clearly haven’t been threatened ... yet.

That’s right. "Yet" is the big word and assumption here. But we have already seen some notable shifts in large AI spenders, as I enunciated earlier.

Adobe's AI Future Is Now Up In The Air

And AI budgets are expected to become even more pronounced and expanding moving forward, with a clearly growing allocation to AI spend as part of overall tech outlay.

I think there are enough surveys out there reminding us that the disruptive wave isn’t merely theoretical.

And for Adobe, the questions about whether the current margins are still sustainable will now be front and center from here.

I think Narayen is likely leaving the necessary details for Adobe’s transformation to the next boss, although I’m still keen to find out what the company has in store for us at the upcoming April summit.

While ADBE’s incredibly profitable money printing machine should help mitigate a further decline in its well-battered multiples, it’s too early for us to ascertain a bottom.

Nevertheless, I do note that the company is seeing solid traction in using its freemium model to encourage conversion.

Although questions about whether it will slow ARR monetization will continue to dominate the investing discourse, which I believe is likely inevitable.

Adobe thinks that the current transition follows a similar adoption cadence to that of Acrobat, where paid conversion should naturally follow later.

I think this is where the conundrum really is.

Investors need to judge whether Adobe’s AI offerings are strong or robust enough to spur the conversion, but it’s challenging to put a hard number to the monetization metrics right now.

So, I think cautious investors will probably assess that unless Adobe offers tremendous value in terms of pricing to its customers, it might be hard to convince people to pay up, right?

So this freemium model will continue to face substantive pressure, and I don’t think these questions will be resolved anytime soon as the company experiments with the different value propositions for its pricing model.

If Canva and folks (plus the AI disruptors) are ultimately making significant inroads into Adobe’s business, keeping the margins at such high levels no longer seems to make much sense (to me, at least).

Adobe’s gargantuan ARR of $26 billion augments the enterprise opportunity to convince customers to stay and spend. The platform stickiness is there, but for the non-AI use cases, maybe.

For AI spending, it's really hard to ascertain right now. And that's where the main confusion is at, as the market needs to decide on the appropriate multiples to value ADBE.

Buy Or Sell ADBE Stock?

However, the observation remains that Adobe’s Firefly hasn’t quite performed up to par given the current restrictions that have hampered its adoption.

Third party integration has kind of resolved the interim challenges. Yet, the flip side of it also suggests that it’s hard for investors to consider a premium multiple at this rate.

If the creative software market is going to become more fragmented, it still needs to fend off its faster-growing rivals making gains into its segments.

Such intense competition could be inimical to Adobe's prized margins in the long run

For now, while I acknowledge that much pessimism has been priced in, the ambiguities relating to the management change won't be resolved anytime soon.

And the competitive challenges also don’t look like there’s a quick fix in the near term.

In some ways, Adobe really does seem like it’s mired and stuck between a rock and a hard place.

It still needs to convince investors that it could keep whipping out those highly prized margins, although I’m quite skeptical about it.

Which is why I believe the market probably wants us to think that ADBE’s low forward earnings multiples aren’t totally unjustified.

With its competitive moat under threat, I’m not exactly sure how quickly buyers will return to help turn it around anytime soon.

Staying on the sidelines and monitoring the developments for now isn’t just prudent, but also apt, in my opinion.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp: An Income Play With Covered Calls
data.description:
Introduction

U.S. regional banks such as Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) are holding onto modest gains so far in 2026, outperforming the broader iShares US Financials ETF (IYF). In my opinion, this makes sense since regional banks' exposure to the U.S. economy acts as a tailwind amid energy price volatility, which is primarily impacting energy-importing economies overseas. In contrast, larger U.S. financials, which have more exposure to countries outside the United States, may record weaker EPS growth in 2026.

Against this backdrop, I think FITB shares offer an interesting income play with covered calls. The investment thesis behind the covered call trade can be summarized as:

  • FITB stock trades at a circa 9% forward P/E premium to regional bank peers, arguably presenting limited upside in the near term.
  • At the same time, analysts expect EPS growth to accelerate to roughly 20% in 2027 as benefits from the merger with Comerica kick in.
  • Meanwhile, selling out-of-the-money covered calls provides current income and downside protection against risks, the principal of which is a potential recession in the United States.

2025 Results Recap

FITB delivered EPS of $3.53/share in 2025, a 12.4% Y/Y increase relative to 2024. The principal drivers were a lower share count (circa 2% contribution), an improved efficiency ratio of 56.9% (-2.3% Y/Y), and a higher net interest margin of 3.11% (+0.21% Y/Y). This helped offset marginally higher credit costs.

The bank's CET1 capital improved to 10.77% (+0.2% Y/Y), while the loan-to-deposit ratio was little changed at 72% (2024: 73%). As such, despite solid loan growth of 4.7% Y/Y, deposit inflows and retained earnings helped FITB achieve a higher net interest margin.

All in all, from an operating perspective, I think there were no major red flags at FITB, with focus now turning to 2026 and the effects of the merger with Comerica, which closed in Q1 2026.

2026 Outlook

2026 results will be impacted by costs associated with the Comerica merger, with FITB targeting conversion of systems and branches for H2 2026. As a result, analysts expect EPS growth of about 15% to $4.07/share in 2026, accelerating by roughly 20% to $4.90/share in 2027.

This comes as the bank recently lowered its non-interest income outlook for 2026, most likely driven by weakness in capital markets. As a reminder, FITB's Wealth & Asset Management segment accounts for 17% of FITB fees.

All in all, while some merger costs were absorbed in 2025, the Comerica deal will still present a near-term headwind before synergies begin to contribute in 2027.

Potential To Sell Covered Calls

As I write this, FITB shares trade around $47.11, reflecting a 2026 P/E multiple of 11.58x. This represents a circa 9% premium to regional bank peers, which trade at roughly 10.66x their forward earnings. As such, FITB's valuation clearly reflects expectations for an acceleration in earnings growth once merger costs of 2026 begin to fade and 2027 synergies start.

Against this backdrop, I would rate FITB as a "Hold," advising readers to go for a covered call selling strategy over the coming months. Taking note of higher 2027 EPS expectations and a 12-month price target of $56.10/share, I think selling out-of-the-money covered calls would represent the best risk-reward.

On the one hand, selling call options will generate some current income, with a strike price between $50 and $55 most suitable depending on individual preferences for option income versus capital gain potential. On the other hand, this option income will also provide some loss absorption capacity as FITB shares are arguably somewhat expensive in the near term.

Looking at FITB option prices, we see the June 2026 $50 calls trading at about $1.80 per contract, reduced to $0.53 per contract for the $55 calls. As such, selling out-of-the-money covered calls could generate incremental income of about ~1%-3% for 2.5 months, respectively.

More conservative readers may want to consider the September 2026 calls, which offer somewhat lower option income per month but larger upfront downside protection, as well as smaller rollover risk (which could materialize if we see a substantial change in the price of FITB stock between June/September).

To conclude, while FITB stock arguably offers limited upside in the near term, it remains an interesting opportunity for investors with a 2027 horizon. Meanwhile, selling covered calls can generate incremental income for holders of FITB stock.

Risks

While the covered call strategy can provide downside protection, it still comes with several risks. Firstly, a severe downturn in the U.S. economy may push up credit costs at FITB, dashing any hopes for EPS growth driven by the merger with Comerica. These risks have recently increased somewhat, with Goldman Sachs (GS) increasing the recession probability to 30% over the next 12 months.

If you do decide to go for the covered call selling strategy, you should note that the position may result in a near-term mark-to-market loss if FITB stock volatility increases. Likewise, investors may miss out on capital gain upside beyond their chosen strike price. As such, if one is more bullish on FITB stock, limiting the upside with covered calls does not make sense.

The third principal risk to consider is that the realization of merger synergies depends on a successful integration of Comerica branches and systems in H2 2026. As such, execution risk about the integration should also be on the radar for investors.

Conclusion

FITB stock has managed to post modest gains so far in 2026, in line with regional bank peers. This comes as larger U.S. financials take a hit due to their higher valuations and exposure to overseas markets suffering from energy price volatility.

Analysts expect FITB's earnings growth to accelerate from 2026 to 2027 as the bank puts merger costs associated with the combination with Comerica behind it. That said, FITB's somewhat elevated valuation relative to peers arguably presents limited upside in the near term.

I think this backdrop creates an opportunity to sell out-of-the-money covered calls, providing some current income and downside protection as FITB works on the Comerica integration.

Key risks in the investment case include a potential recession in the United States, mark-to-market losses and limited upside from selling covered calls, as well as execution risks related to the Comerica systems integration.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Asset Class Scoreboard: March 2026
data.description:
March 2026 brought a broad pullback across most asset classes to close out the first quarter. World stocks fell -8.03% and U.S. real estate dropped -6.37%, while U.S. stocks declined -4.93%. These losses pushed U.S. stocks into negative territory for the year at -4.36%, while world stocks held onto a slim +2.01% year-to-date gain despite the difficult month.

Commodities stood as the clear outlier on the March scoreboard, surging +24.23%. Building on gains of +10.49% in January and +1.88% in February, commodities now lead all asset classes by a wide margin with a year-to-date return of +39.84% through the end of Q1.

Managed futures slipped just -0.53% in March. One of the smaller declines on the scoreboard during a month when most risk assets fell sharply. Their year-to-date return remains at +7.35%, placing them second on the 2026 scoreboard behind Commodities and well ahead of the rest of the field.

Hedge funds and bonds also finished March in the red, declining -2.24% and -1.74%, respectively. Both remain positive for the year, though narrowly, hedge funds at +1.82% and bonds at just +0.05%. Cash continued its steady pace with another +0.31% monthly return, bringing its Q1 total to +0.92%. U.S. real estate, despite March’s decline, finished Q1 with a modest +1.00% year-to-date gain.

As Q1 closes, commodities hold a commanding lead on the 2026 scoreboard, with managed futures in a clear second place. U.S. stocks remain the only asset class in negative territory for the year.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

data.title: Cellebrite: A Rare SaaS Play Where AI Is A Tailwind, Not A Threat
data.description:
In the current AI environment, I am certainly not the most optimistic when it comes to SaaS stocks. In fact, I recently analyzed UiPath (PATH) and as much as I don't think it's a bad company, I think there is a very large uncertainty in the business model and also much more attractive alternatives to ride the same trend.

But I'm also not that guy who thinks that absolutely all SaaS companies are dying businesses and have no moat. I know that there are some good companies that will use AI as something to boost growth and the moat.

And one of them is Cellebrite DI (CLBT). A company that is in a very interesting niche, and the financials are in great shape, all of this for a very reasonable valuation.

Why Cellebrite Is A Good Company

I believe that common sense, or first level thinking, is that Cellebrite is a company that “unlocks” smartphones. Because of this, perhaps the moat and the real opportunity go unnoticed many times.

It's not that this is a wrong view, especially because one of the products really is the “Universal Forensic Extraction Device”, that is, a service to extract physical data from devices like an iPhone. This in itself would already be a small moat, since the police and similar agents could not use pirated software or an “unofficial” alternative; it needs to be something serious, secure, approved, and all that.

But Cellebrite is far from being a company that only unlocks cell phones, discovers passwords, and all that. There is a whole platform, a management behind it. Guardian, for example, is a Cellebrite cloud system. After extracting hundreds of gigabytes from a device of someone who is being investigated, this needs to be stored somewhere. Guardian is the place where everyone involved can access it securely and with various “features” so that everyone has access but only with permissions that are necessary. And in this context these things matter a lot; it's not like a company like Anthropic could likely solve it.

By the way, even if AI could unlock iPhones, extract the gigabytes, and upload it to something like a Google Drive, it probably wouldn't work because of this regulatory barrier. Think that Cellebrite has already been approved by thousands of customers. For each of these thousands of customers, dozens/hundreds of people involved were certified and trained to know and be fit to use Cellebrite software. This, in addition to something regulatory that not just anyone can offer a service like this to replace Cellebrite, is also a gigantic switching cost.

A real example that I found interesting was the Civil Police of Rio Grande do Sul, which is a state in the south of Brazil. In this case, it was mentioned that investigations are increasingly digital because data is increasing, and complexity is increasing, so using this software ends up being something that greatly increases efficiency.

The agent uses Cellebrite software, extracting dozens of gigabytes, sometimes terabytes, through the software. After that, they can do a curation (Pathfinder) to find where the evidence is through the improvements with AI that Cellebrite is making and then use a cloud system.

After all this and seeing that it really works, having trained several of the police officers, what is the chance of this customer switching software? Assuming a scenario that is viable and cheaper, would it still be worth it to use, for instance, independent software to extract data, then use Gemini to filter the data, and then another cloud company to upload the data? Besides not being secure, having to train several police officers on several different platforms is not the optimal scenario.

And well, as I mentioned at the beginning of the article, CLBT's financials are in great shape. This only reinforces that these moats and the prospects for demand are truly solid. Note, for instance, how revenue is growing. Subscription revenue increased 21%, and annual recurring revenue (ARR) as well, already reaching $481 million. The rule of 40 is also very positive here; it's not like a Palantir (PLTR) which is way off the curve, but it's already solid enough, at around 55, given the growth of ~20% with the FCF margin of 34%.

The guidance for 2026 also corroborates this optimism. Cellebrite expects growth of 18% - 19% for ARR, and a stable margin for adj. EBITDA.

In summary, here I think we have a SaaS case that is truly interesting. The addressable market is large and has room to grow. AI is a tailwind because it allows the software to find evidence faster and faster and add more and more value for the customer, and mainly, the company has already built a relevant moat, which is something that allows us to have a higher conviction in the entire case.

Some Uncertainties and Risks

It is difficult to trace a risk as clear for Cellebrite as for other SaaS companies that are being threatened by AI and by other SaaS that are 10x their size. But Cellebrite has a risk and an uncertainty that other SaaS companies don't have, and it's a bit related to ESG.

While regulation is something that is one of Cellebrite's moats, it also ends up being a concern at certain times. For instance, there are operations in countries such as Russia and China that may be delicate/sensitive for some. In itself, this can already be an impediment for some and can even cause the stock to be traded cheaper because of this potential negative perception.

But one can go beyond that, and say that this is also a pragmatic uncertainty, since it can affect financials. If there is heavier regulation by international bodies and certain countries, it is not an impossible scenario where Cellebrite is fined or judged for it. I'm not saying it should be or that this is a likely scenario. I'm saying that Cellebrite is a company present in several countries, and legislation in many regions is something difficult to predict. I am Brazilian, and I know that here there are laws and other judicial decisions that are very controversial, and many were unexpected. So I know it's not an impossible scenario where a judge potentially determines that Cellebrite needs to pay an X amount to the Brazilian government or even prohibit the use of the software in the country.

In short, it is something delicate; execution needs to be very assertive.

Valuation And Dilution

The uncertainties do not stop there. The margin currently is already very good, but the GAAP margin is not that attractive anymore. It is already a healthy margin, but note how stock-based compensation causes net income to go from ~$78 million (GAAP) to $130 million (adjusted).

This is something to observe, because if the shares outstanding continue to increase a lot, it ends up being negative for “real” shareholder value creation. Currently it's not worrying; GAAP net income is already very healthy, and shares outstanding were flat QoQ, although they grew YoY.

This is also related to the valuation and the reason why I consider it a reasonable valuation.

The fwd non-GAAP P/E of 26x, in isolation, seems excellent already. This is because the company is growing the top line at a very appealing pace, and the prospects are sustainable, besides also having the potential to capture operating leverage and grow the bottom line at an even greater pace. For instance, the market expects the P/E for 2028 to fall to ~18x with constant EPS growth.

But more than that, the fwd GAAP P/E is already healthy. Even looking at the last 12 months, the multiple is already close to ~40x (TTM), and with growth and operating leverage, it should become increasingly attractive in the coming years.

Of course, you will find other SaaS companies like SAP (SAP) and Salesforce (CRM) trading at similar or even better multiples, but I don't think they manage to have the appeal that Cellebrite has. As the moat here is larger and the perception of disruption is smaller, it's easier to believe that there really is shareholder value to be created in the next 10 and 20 years, without such a large uncertainty related to disruption.

Bottom Line: A Good SaaS Company

In short, even with the uncertainties, I believe that the Cellebrite case is very promising for compounding. Revenue is very recurring, and uncertainties related to churn, competition, and disruption are low, which makes this compounding even more attractive.

For this reason, I start my coverage with a buy.

data.title: The Carnival Stock Price Plunge Is An Opportunity
data.description:
Much has gone down with cruise operator Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) (CUK) since I last checked on it in December. From then until early February, its price rose by no less than 30%. Cut to now, however, and it's back to where I last left it. So here, I discuss why CCL has nosedived and move on to why there's now even more upside to it now.

Why the Carnival Stock Nosedived

While the stock had already started gently coming off after touching multi-year highs in early February, likely on profit taking, it truly slumped following the start of the US-Iran war.

It has lost some 20% of its value since the end of February, as a result, making its decline the second biggest after Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd. (NCLH) (see chart below). The outsized impact on cruises was to be anticipated for the following four reasons:

#1. Inflation Risk Can Dent Consumer Demand

The most apparent macroeconomic impact of the war is, of course, the oil price shock that has tipped crude prices to over $100/bbl. Even though the Fed's latest economic projections indicate that inflation risk is contained so far, consumers are beginning to feel uncertain.

This is evident in the latest consumer confidence survey results. For example, the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index dropped for the first time in three months. While the Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index did better, even in this case, consumers optimism about the future is waning. If a spending pullback occurs, consumer discretionary services like cruises could well be impacted.

#2. Carnival Brands Cancel Some Cruises

The war impacts supply-side dynamics too. Two of Carnival's brands, Costa Cruises and Aida Cruises, have cancelled trips to the Middle East, citing unpredictability in the region. While these trips have been redirected elsewhere, indicating that revenues might not be materially impacted, the cancellations could still have had a sentimental impact on price.

#3. Fuel Costs Set to Rise

Fuel prices are not an unimportant component of Carnival's total costs already, constituting 8% of total costs and close to 7% of the total revenues in the financial year 2025 (FY25, year ending November 2025). This number is set to rise as the company forecasts fuel costs to increase by 19% YoY in FY26, landing at $2.15 billion. This big expected increase is especially disappointing after their 10% YoY drop in 2025.

#4. Profit Guidance Reduced

Finally, Carnival expects negative spillovers on profits in FY26 now, even as purchased fuel prices are forecast to drop over the remainder of the year. The company slashed its adjusted EBITDA guidance by 5.7% and adjusted net income projections by 11% for FY26 (see slide below). It's some comfort that both numbers would stay essentially flat from 2025, but that's only assuming that things don't get worse from here.

Why CCL's Price Can Rise

Dismal as the situation appears, there's actually a fair bit going for the Carnival stock as well. Here are four supporting factors:

#1. Attractive Market Multiples

Assuming that Carnival's adjusted net income comes in at its latest guidance in FY26, CCL's forward non-GAAP P/E comes to 11.6x. This is lower than the level of 12.1x in December 2025, after which the stock saw a significant uptick, as noted at the beginning.

Its P/E also compares favorably to the average of cruise stocks CCL, NCLH, Royal Caribbean (RCL), and Viking Holdings (VIK), at 14.2x, indicating the potential for 25-30% upside. It's also worth mentioning that of all cruise stocks, only NCLH has a smaller forward P/E compared to CCL at 8.2x.

Similarly, CCL's forward EV/EBITDA, at 8.4x, is also ahead only of NCLH, at 8.3x. This figure, too, compares well against the sector average of 11.3x, indicating over 30% upside. The EV/EBITDA figure is important for cruise stocks, which saw massive debt increases during the pandemic that are still being paid off.

#2. Positive Medium-Term Earnings Outlook

Along with its recent Q1 FY26 results, the company also released its outlook for up to 2029. It expects the adjusted EPS to increase by over 50% between 2025 and 2029. Assuming that it rises by exactly 50%, the number would stand at $3.4 in the year, resulting in a forward P/E of just 7.6x for the end year.

#3. Continued Improvement in Debt Conditions

Additionally, it expects a significant reduction in the net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio to 2.75x by 2029, from 3.4x in 2025. This is an important indication of its balance sheet's health after the company's net debt ballooned by almost 3x between 2019 and 2022 during the pandemic.

To provide some perspective, as the company turned adjusted EBITDA positive in 2023, its net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA was at a high of ~7x. The figure has halved since, partly due to an 11% drop in net debt levels since, but more due to a 69% rise in EBITDA. To this extent, the latest guidance reduction is a downer, but it's still heartening that Carnival sees a drop in the ratio over time.

#4. Dividends Restart

The company has also cautiously started paying dividends again. It's only one of the two cruise companies to do so, the other one being RCL. Its forward dividend yield of 2.34% is slightly ahead of RCL's 2.1%, another positive for it. With the dividend payout ratio of 27%, the number looks sustainable, even if the adjusted EPS projections are reduced further.

What's Next?

Carnival's timing of the medium-term outlook is the key confidence builder in the stock right now as the short-term continues to look uncertain. And this outlook certainly looks good, especially after the stock has dropped in the recent past.

If the pre-war months are anything to go by, CCL can rally once the situation returns to normal. We don't know when that will happen. It's entirely possible that if the tensions drag on, for 2026, the company's guidance could be reduced further as inflation becomes a bigger challenge and consumer demand is impacted. Even then, though, over the next few years, the stock will stand to gain, and significantly. I'm retaining a Buy rating.

data.title: $100 Oil Won't Sink The U.S. Economy
data.description:
Introduction

Last week, the entire nation and the market expected US President Donald Trump to de-escalate the Iran war.

That did not happen.

On Saturday, Trump wrote in a social media post, saying, “Time is running out—48 hours before all hell will rain down on them,” as the war enters its fifth week and Iran continues to reject US demands.

In order to avert a situation where the US hits Iran’s power plants on Tuesday, which would result in a dramatic escalation in the war and oil prices, the two countries and regional mediators are negotiating a potential 45-day ceasefire that could pave the way for ending the war. These latest discussions are centered on a two-phased deal, where the first phase would be a 45-day ceasefire to allow further negotiations, and the second phase would be a deal to end the war.

With the US 10-year Treasury yields climbing as high as 4.4% mid-last week before closing at 4.35% for the week, along with oil prices measured by Brent crude (CO1:COM) climbing 1.6% for the week to $108/barrel, the markets are starting to price in a “Trump risk premium” as policy uncertainty and geopolitical tensions rise, according to economist Robin Brooks.

However, the way I see it, even with $100+ per barrel of oil prices, the US economy won’t likely enter a recession for reasons I will explain below. I also believe that with midterm elections now just 8 months away, a prolonged war with higher energy prices and rising interest rates is not in President Trump’s best interests, increasing the likelihood of a faster resolution to the Iran war over the next several weeks.

The US Economy Is Well Positioned To Handle $100+ Oil Prices

First, the energy intensity in the US has been declining, reflecting improvements in energy efficiency and structural changes in the economy, as you can see below. The energy intensity is a measure of the amount of energy used to produce a unit of economic input. Based on analysis from Capital Economics, a sustained $100+ oil price would only reduce real GDP growth by approximately 0.2% to 0.3%, as the reduction in non-energy spending from consumers will be offset by the positive effects of stronger income given the US's position as a modest net energy exporter.

Second, gasoline and energy goods represented only 2% of total consumer spending in the fourth quarter of 2025, according to Bureau of Economic Analysis data. This is the lowest percentage over the past 80 years, barring the pandemic period of 2020-2021. While the level will rise (as a percentage of total consumer spending) if oil prices remain elevated at current levels, what’s important to note is that household balance sheets at an aggregate level remain extremely strong, thus positioning the average US consumer to handle these oil prices well.

Third, while Goldman Sachs analysts predict that headline inflation will rise by 0.2 percentage points for every 10% increase in oil prices, I don’t believe that the Fed will likely raise interest rates to combat a supply-driven inflation surge, especially as long as oil prices remain below 2022 levels.

Fourth, wartime spending could also help offset the oil burden, as Fundstrat’s Tom Lee pointed out. According to his estimates, wartime spending has averaged $32B a month in inflation-adjusted terms over the last 7 major US conflicts, which adds directly to US GDP. He also claims that in order for oil prices to fully offset the stimulus from defense expenditures to GDP, oil would need to cross over $125, 15% higher than current levels. Even in that case, a slowdown in non-energy-related consumer spending could be partially offset by an increase in revenue from oil exports, given the US's position as a net energy exporter.

In other words, even though $100+ oil prices are a drag on consumer spending, particularly on the lower-income households, the US economy as a whole is unlikely to enter a recession because of this.

Even when we look at the projections from the Federal Reserve at their FOMC meeting last month, we can see that they expect the US economy to grow 2.4% for the full year, while analysts from Goldman Sachs have revised their expectations lower from 2.8% to 2.6%, still far away from a recession.

What I’m Watching Out For

What was also encouraging was last week’s jobs report, which showed a significant rebound in hiring that exceeded analyst expectations.

During March, the US economy added 178,000 jobs, the biggest increase in 15 months, while the unemployment rate fell slightly from 4.4% in February to 4.3% in March.

Meanwhile, earnings expectations for the S&P 500 are being revised higher for Q1 as well as for FY26, with Information Technology and Energy seeing the highest concentration of upward revisions from strong AI-related spending across industries and rising oil prices.

Despite markets beginning to price in a “Trump risk premium” given the political risk tied to Trump’s policy approach around trade, fiscal expansion, and geopolitical tensions, most investment banks and research firms are maintaining their year-end target for the S&P 500 that stands at least 15% higher than the current levels.

This is not how a typical recession unfolds.

Having said that, I will point out two major risk areas that I’m currently monitoring.

The first area of concern is centered on the matter of energy inequality in the US. While the US economy as a whole is well positioned to handle $100+ oil prices, this could disproportionately hit low- and middle-income consumers, as energy comprises 17% of lower-income spending as a share of pre-tax income, based on research from JP Morgan Asset Management. According to Meera Pandit, Global Market Strategist at JP Morgan Asset Management, this could jeopardize some of the positive impacts of elevated tax refunds but raise the odds of additional fiscal stimulus to support the consumer in advance of the midterm elections.

Meanwhile, a prolonged period of $100+ oil prices is negative for overall liquidity conditions. We are already seeing the spreads in junk as well as corporate bonds (relative to the 10Y US Treasury) rise, which makes it harder for corporations to borrow money as well as refinance their existing debt, thus increasing the odds of defaults.

While the corporate debt levels are much lower today as a percentage of GDP compared to their peak in 2008, as I discussed in one of my previous posts, a worsening of liquidity conditions will certainly strain business investments, which will in turn limit the growth rate of their corporate profits.

Summing It All Up

While a recession is not my base case scenario at the moment for the US economy, I certainly believe that an expedited resolution to the Iran war is necessary at the moment.

The Kobeissi Letter wrote this post outlining a 10-step framework of President Trump’s conflict playbook based on a “clear pattern” in the way President Trump negotiates and exerts pressure on both US enemies and allies when he is attempting to accomplish an economic or military objective.

They highlighted these three scenarios that could emerge in the coming weeks, which I fully agree with.

In the first scenario, escalation will briefly intensify, pushing oil prices higher and the S&P 500 (SPY) lower before a drastic shift in language as we reach a deal much faster than markets anticipate.

In the second scenario, the conflict continues in a controlled way over the next few weeks without dramatic escalation until a resolution is reached. In this case, both oil prices and the S&P 500 will remain range-bound at least until a resolution is reached later in the month.

In the third scenario, the escalation broadens significantly, which would lead to oil prices surging beyond the 2022 levels to possibly even $150 levels. This would undeniably increase the odds of a recession in the US economy, leading to a broader de-rating of equities.

Given that we are just 8 months away from midterm elections, a prolonged war with higher energy prices and lower S&P 500 levels is not in President Trump’s best interests. This is why I do not believe the third scenario above has a particularly high likelihood of playing out.

The way I see it, pessimism has broadly become embedded in the S&P 500 at the moment as markets are pricing in a more prolonged period of geopolitical uncertainty, otherwise known as the “Trump risk premium." As a result, despite corporate earnings growth expectations remaining resilient, the forward PE ratio of the S&P 500 has dipped from 22.00 at the end of 2025 to 19.8, which is lower than its 5-year average.

From here onwards, if we follow either of the first two scenarios for de-escalation, we should see a peaking in both the oil prices and the 10-year US Treasury yields, which would lead to a meaningful bounce in the index. Over the longer term, I continue to remain bullish on the S&P 500, even though there could be meaningful volatility in the short term.

data.title: Invesco Global Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The fund outperformed its benchmark for the quarter

Class A shares outperformed the MSCI ACWI Growth Index during the quarter, ranking in the 10th percentile (31 of 314) of the Morningstar Global Large-Stock Growth category.

2 US stocks lagged non-US stocks; growth stocks underperformed

Global equities rose, led by emerging market and European equities, as Asia's technology-driven gains offset late-quarter weakness in US technology stocks.

3 Positioned for upside in 2026

Looking to 2026, we believe a supportive macroeconomic backdrop and easing monetary policy support our constructive outlook for global equities. We believe the most compelling opportunities are in high quality companies and leading AI innovators.

Investment objective

The fund seeks capital appreciation.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M) 9,109.32

Portfolio managers

John Delano

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Global equities were positive for the quarter, with international equities outperforming the US as late-quarter concerns about elevated technology valuations appeared to temper AI-driven momentum. Emerging markets led due to a broad Asian technology rally and robust results in South Korea; China lagged due to weak economic data and currency strength. Europe also delivered solid returns, supported by a stronger euro, improving manufacturing trends and expectations of fiscal stimulus in Germany.
  • Looking to 2026, we maintain a constructive outlook for global equities, supported by reduced trade policy uncertainty, firmer growth forecasts and expected monetary easing led by the US Federal Reserve, despite cooling in the US labor market. Risks such as slower growth, renewed inflation and geopolitics have persisted, but we do not expect them to materially alter our long-term investment views.
  • In this environment, we still favor high quality companies with good balance sheets, attractive profit margins and solid cash-generation capabilities. We see greater vulnerabilities in private markets and lower quality AI-themed companies reliant on debt-funded spending. We believe public markets will keep rewarding data center hyperscalers and AI innovators able to self-fund investment, with the most compelling long-term opportunities among companies owning leading semiconductor intellectual property or advanced large language models.

Portfolio positioning

We initiated three new positions during the quarter.

Nintendo (NTDOY) is a Japan-based video game and entertainment company known for major consoles including Game Boy, Wii, Nintendo Switch and recently launched Switch 2. Its franchises such as Mario, Pokémon and Zelda, in our view, support strong monetization through merchandise, films and theme parks. The company benefited from robust Switch 2 sales and growing recurring revenue from Nintendo Accounts and Switch Online subscriptions.

Las Vegas Sands (LVS) develops and operates integrated resorts with luxury hotels, casinos and convention centers, primarily in Macao and Singapore. The company has benefited from strong recoveries in these markets and has continued to upgrade its portfolio, including adding a new high-end hotel tower at Marina Bay Sands in Singapore, which we expect to drive future growth. The company recently raised its dividend and expanded share buybacks.

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) is a multinational holding company that owns businesses including GEICO, BNSF Railway, Duracell and Dairy Queen, along with major equity stakes in American Express, Coca-Cola and Apple. Leadership transitioned on January 1st when longtime CEO Warren Buffett stepped down and Greg Abel assumed the role. We initiated a position due to what we saw as attractive valuation and diversified economic exposure. We funded the position by selling Zoetis.

We sold three positions during the quarter.

NICE (NICE), an enterprise software company, issued a weaker 2026 profit outlook due to heavy AI investment and declining revenue. The company may sell its strong Actimize division to fund the acquisition of AI startup Cognigy, a move that appears to be viewed cautiously by investors. We saw rising AI-related competitive risk, particularly from Anthropic, and sold the position.

Ferrari (RACE), an Italian luxury car manufacturer, was sold after a management meeting reinforced our concerns about the company's focus on personalization and hybrids, which historically have lower resale values and demand. Although Ferrari plans to shift back toward more internal combustion models, growth is expected to remain limited during the transition.

Zoetis (ZTS), a global animal health company, reported weak results tied to macroeconomic pressures and fewer veterinary visits, an explanation not in line with competitors. After Zoetis cut its revenue forecast due to softer demand, we sold the position and redeployed capital into opportunities we considered more attractive.

Performance highlights

Stock selection in health care, information technology (IT) and financials added to relative return, as did an overweight in health care and an underweight in IT.

Stock selection in communication services detracted the most from relative results. The fund's real estate holdings outperformed those of the benchmark but an overweight in the sector detracted from relative return.

Contributors to performance

Below are the largest contributors to relative return for the quarter:

Alphabet had a strong quarter with revenue topping $100 billion, driven by AI demand, Google Cloud growth and momentum in search advertising. TPU (tensor processing unit) chip sales and the success of Gemini Pro 3, in our view, reinforced Alphabet's AI leadership and it remains the fund's largest holding.

Lam Research beat revenue and earnings expectations on strong demand for advanced semiconductor tools, as its etch and deposition technologies gained traction across next-generation chip designs.

Microsoft stock declined on concerns about rising AI capital spending and slower product uptake. The fund is underweight relative to the benchmark because we remain cautious about its valuation despite AI investment capacity. The underweight added to relative return.

Eli Lilly had a robust quarter due to soaring demand for GLP-1 obesity drugs. Management raised guidance and the stock reached a $1 trillion market cap. Amid growing optimism for its upcoming oral GLP-1 drug, we trimmed the position after strong gains.

Taiwan Semiconductor continued to benefit from robust AI chip demand, beating revenue and profit estimates. It is investing heavily in advanced manufacturing in an effort to preserve its technological lead and meet long-term customer needs.

Detractors from performance

Below are the largest detractors from relative return for the quarter:

Meta Platform stock fell on concerns about AI spending and a one-time tax charge, though we believe returns on AI investment remain strong. Our thesis is intact, but we trimmed the position in an effort to balance risk and reward. Meta remains the fund's second largest holding.

JD.com faced pressures from its food delivery expansion, rising competition and China's weak consumer trends. Given risks in the food delivery market, we trimmed the position.

Apple is not held by the fund due to its premium valuation, supply-chain risks and slower progress in key technologies like AI. However, strong iPhone 17 demand and services growth boosted the stock during the quarter and the underweight detracted.

DLF has benefited from rising affluence and long-term structural trends in India. The stock fell on sector weakness but remains a high-conviction holding for us.

SAP declined amid revenue that lagged expectations, a soft cloud backlog and AI-related concerns. Though it has been a strong performer for the fund, we trimmed the position due to valuation.

data.title: Unusual Machines: How To Play The Drone Boom Without Betting On A Single Platform
data.description:
Thesis

Unusual Machines, Inc. (UMAC) is an early-stage but very well-capitalized supplier to a pretty rapidly emerging, policy-driven U.S. drone supply chain. We’re seeing a lot of regulatory barriers, like NDAA and FCC bans, make way for a meaningful domestic components market. As you know, drone warfare and counter-drone systems are shifting toward high-volume, attritable models. So the demand for a lot of core components like motors, cameras, and FPV systems is likely to scale exponentially over the next 10 years. The way I see it, Unusual is trying to position itself to supply that demand across the different drone platforms rather than betting on a single winner. The good news is that they hold a pretty strong balance sheet and are now starting to accelerate their enterprise revenue mix, with about 81% in 4Q25. Production capacity is also growing rapidly, with the company having a credible path to scaling revenue toward $100 million if execution holds, as I’ll explain. So, at current valuations, the buy case here is that Unusual becomes a critical infrastructure layer in the U.S. drone ecosystem. Upside should be driven by successful capacity ramp and sustained demand, while downside is mainly around execution risk during scaling.

FY25 Review

Unusual’s FY25 is showing us a company in the early but very aggressive phase of scaling. Growth and capacity-building are clearly taking priority over near-term profitability. Revenue more than doubled year-over-year to $11.2 million, up above 101%, with a particularly sharp inflection coming in Q4, which added $4.9 million alone. That quarter is up 144% year over year and 133% sequentially. We can put that surge down to a classic hardware ramp pattern, with the company doubling its headcount in 3Q25 and expanding facilities significantly, and revenue followed with a bit of a lag in 4Q25. As for gross margins, they still held relatively strong at about 35% for the year, which I felt was pretty notable given the transition from retail toward enterprise sales. There were also the early-stage inefficiencies of new production lines to deal with. So for me, this suggests some underlying pricing power and a favorable mix shift here, even with the company beginning domestic manufacturing of a few key components like motors and FPV headsets.

In saying that, the income statement is where we can see the cost of this rapid expansion. Operating expenses surged to $29.1 million compared to the $18.5 million we saw back in 2024. Now, this was mainly driven by general and administrative costs, with about $15.6 million in stock-based compensation. The result is that the company posted a net loss of $19.2 million, but it’s worth noting that this was an improvement from the $32.0 million loss in 2024. What’s important here is that much of the loss is non-cash and that management actually emphasizes a much smaller adjusted loss of around $0.9 million in 4Q25. So that would frame the business as closer to breakeven on an operational basis. Cash flow tells me a more grounded story, however. You see, the company burned about $21.2 million in operating cash, which was largely due to inventory build and working capital expansion. But still, this was more than offset by a hefty $157.8 million sum raised through equity financings. This left them with a very strong balance sheet at the end of FY25: just over $103 million in cash, $39 million in short-term investments, and no debt.

Strategically speaking, I see the company positioning itself to capture a potentially large and policy-driven market opportunity, especially in NDAA-compliant drone components. Just to touch on their TAM briefly, as per management, there is a near-term addressable market of $90 million in 2026 and $250 million in 2027 tied to U.S. drone production targets. So this would imply significant headroom relative to current revenue.

I think the big execution challenge here will be matching the anticipated demand with disciplined scaling. After all, they are a drone parts manufacturer, so there was a current demand to avoid overproduction and margin compression. We can expect near-term margins to dip, especially in 1Q26, due to startup inefficiencies in new manufacturing lines. But overall, the company is targeting a return to about 40% gross margins over time. So Unusual seems quite well-capitalized but still very much an unproven growth story. We have strong top-line momentum and balance sheet strength, being offset by execution risk and some reliance on external funding. What they need is to convert capacity expansion into sustained, profitable revenue growth.

How Do They Make Money?

Unusual essentially makes money by positioning itself as a supplier to the drone industry. So rather than primarily selling finished drones, their main business is more around designing, manufacturing, and selling critical components that go inside the drones themselves. A big part of this is, of course, for FPV (first-person-view) systems. So these would include components like cameras, video transmitters (VTX), flight controllers, electronic speed controllers (ESCs), motors, and even pilot goggles, all of which are very necessary to build or operate a drone in a military environment. For example, a defense contractor assembling a drone might buy a number of parts from Unusual Machines. A Fat Shark HDO+ headset for the pilot to see through the drone, an Aura FPV camera to be the drone’s eye, a VTX so they can transmit video back, and a flight controller with ESC to control movement and motors. Now, these are not optional accessories you can slap on a drone; they’re actually the core electronic and sensory systems that make FPV drones very functional in a military or even hobbyist environment.

As for the business model, it is mainly built around two key brands: Fat Shark (hardware/IP) and Rotor Riot (distribution/community). If we start with Fat Shark, this is where they develop high-performance hardware like ultra-low latency FPV goggles and imaging systems, while Rotor Riot, on the other hand, acts as both an e-commerce marketplace and demand engine. So not just selling Unusual’s products but also third-party drone parts to a pretty large, engaged pilot community. That setup gives them a dual revenue model with direct-to-consumer retail involving hobbyists buying drones, parts, and gear and enterprise/government sales, which make the bulk of component orders to drone manufacturers and defense programs.

As you know, increasingly, the second is the strategic focus here. We’ve seen multi-million dollar orders for NDAA-compliant components going into U.S.-assembled drones in recent times, so Unusual would like to be in a better position to catch these tailwinds. I think their edge here is clearly regulation and geopolitics. You see, most drone supply chains are dominated by Chinese companies, but U.S. government rules such as NDAA compliance and the Blue UAS Framework sort of carve a niche for these players in that they require secure, non-Chinese components and are often willing to pay up big for them. Which is why Unusual is spending so much building a hefty catalog of fully compliant, U.S.-aligned components.

Where Demand Will Come From

In terms of sales momentum, there is definitely a strong case that Unusual is positioning itself not as a drone company but as a foundational infrastructure provider for a somewhat new kind of warfare and security economy. So I think scale, cost, and supply chain control matter more here than individual platforms. If we take their FPV drones and counter-drone systems, I see a future leading more towards conflict or even civilian protection becoming a high-volume, attritable systems game. We can expect thousands or millions of cheaper drones to be constantly produced, deployed, and eventually destroyed. As per management, they described the market as supply-constrained through 2027, with demand coming mainly from real-world conflicts like Ukraine/Middle East and also from programs like Drone Dominance from the U.S. The latter alone would require hundreds of thousands of drones annually to get pushed on the defense market. So instead of building those drones, what Unusual has here is a picks-and-shovels layer. So, motors, which are currently 15,000/month, scaling toward 100,000 plus, FPV headsets, and soon batteries and cameras are all components every drone manufacturer needs regardless of design.

Now, this is pretty crucial since it allows them to sell to hundreds of customers without betting on a single winning platform, as many drone manufacturers have to do, such as Red Cat. Red Cat, for example, has had to be very innovative in their drone design approach, building different platforms for different use cases and even branching into unmanned sea vessels to secure more demand. Management has also explicitly hinted at this in that there is no customer concentration, but rather a broad design-in strategy. So, in building up their supply chain, what they really need is a regulatory moat to help lock in their position. We’ve seen FCC bans, tariffs, and entity list restrictions that have essentially forced a $3 billion to $5 billion domestic components market to emerge. Foreign suppliers, especially Chinese, are becoming structurally excluded, and over the next few years, I expect the space to be filled. Unusual is leaning into this space very early by onshoring production. They’ve raised $158 million and are set to rapidly scale facilities, from 6,900 to 62,500 sq ft, and 19 to 140-plus employees. Now, this should help with a first-mover advantage in a market where capacity itself is the bottleneck, not innovative design or demand. The real bet here, and also the key risk, becomes their execution. We’ve heard from management that hardware scaling has caused some lagging revenues and margin volatility and that they’re aggressively pre-buying inventory and building capacity ahead of demand. So if they succeed, the model is very powerful, especially in a world where every military base, grid, or industrial asset may at least need some kind of layered drone defense, including drone-on-drone interception. What I’m getting at here is that Unusual doesn’t need to win the drone war; they just need to supply the parts to everyone fighting it. That’s how they will capture value across the entire ecosystem as volumes explode, in my view.

Valuation

Unusual is currently trading at some very aggressive, growth-heavy valuation metrics. They brought in about $11.2 million in 2025 revenue and hold an enterprise value of about $510 million. The stock is valued at roughly 45.5x trailing EV/sales and 31.6x price to sales. These are multiples that are 10x to 13x higher than the sector median, around 3x. Even if we look forward, the valuation only compresses to about 19x EV to sales, which still implies a hefty premium here. The only reasonable metric on the surface is the price to book, which sits at about 2.9x, but keep in mind that’s largely inflated by the recent equity raises that boosted the cash sum.

What the market is essentially pricing in here is not just strong growth, but almost near-perfect execution on a very large future opportunity. At these kinds of multiples, investors are assuming that Unusual can scale from that $11 million figure in revenue today to something on the order of $100 million in the near-to-medium term, while still maintaining solid margins that management targets at around 40%. Becoming at least operationally profitable would also be priced in here. I think the valuation also embeds a bit of belief that the company will become a critical, possibly dominant U.S. supplier of NDAA-compliant drone components, with the main benefits coming from regulatory tailwinds and defense demand. What I’m trying to say is that the stock is being priced more like a strategic infrastructure layer in a future domestic drone supply chain than a small-cap hardware startup. So that leaves very little room for error, and any slowdown in demand or in their aggressive scaling of manufacturing could compress multiples significantly here.

Looking Ahead

As for what's next, Unusual seems pretty well positioned as a foundational supplier going forward. We heard from CEO Allan Evans’ recent interview that the company is running at essentially full capacity with demand for its components over the next 18-month period. That was an impressive talk and definitely contributed to the stock run-up this week. I think it goes to show the urgency and scale of production that's really needed here. The main thing is that the company’s growth isn’t dependent on any single conflict, and while those wars have accelerated adoption, I think Unusual would still continue scaling aggressively. What those conflicts have really done is shown the need for more drones going forward, not just in the time period of conflicts. So this kind of operational momentum and that expanding manufacturing capacity tell me they're set to capture a pretty durable, high-volume market here. For investors looking for exposure to the drone ecosystem here without betting on a single finished drone platform or portfolio, Unusual can offer that.

data.title: Alphabet: Still Not Too Late To Jump On The 16%+ Growth Train
data.description:
It's hard to claim the market underestimates Alphabet Inc. (GOOG, GOOGL, GOOG:CA), aka Google, with the stock nearly doubling over the past year, significantly outperforming the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and its peers.

However, I'm going to claim exactly that. Analyst estimates and the recent 20% pullback tell me that the market is way off on Alphabet's "New" growth trajectory in the post-Gemini 2.5 era.

I argue there's a lot of meat left on the bone here and maintain Google as my largest position, with a Strong Buy rating.

The Investment Thesis

Over the past year, market sentiment towards Alphabet shifted sharply, from perhaps the most significant AI loser to one of the clearest AI winners.

I predicted that last year's second quarter was going to be the tipping point, as all the baseless bearish arguments were going to be met with reality - Google Search wasn't dying; in fact, it was accelerating.

Today, the setup is quite different. Google is tagged as a clear AI winner, and there are good reasons for it. However, there's still significant underestimated upside in the Google stock, which is why I remain extremely bullish and maintain Alphabet as my largest position.

The upside should come from three angles - Underestimated Growth Trajectory; Nearing FCF Inflection, and; Two Huge "Free" Options.

Underestimated Growth Trajectory

I expect Alphabet to deliver accelerating revenue growth over the next few years, at a minimum of 16%, while consensus forecasts a slowdown.

It's rare for analysts, mainly sell-side analysts who are the ones behind the consensus we're all relying on, to underwrite stable or accelerating revenue growth. Usually, they forecast the coming year and automatically assume a gradual deceleration in the following years.

That's where thorough individual long-term investors should get their edge.

All up, I estimate Alphabet's non-cloud businesses' growth will exceed 15%, and Google Cloud's will exceed 40%, for the foreseeable future, resulting in a 16%+ growth trajectory.

Search & Other

Starting with Search revenues, which represent ~56% of Alphabet sales. Google exited 2025 at a 17% growth rate vs. 13% for the full year. This is almost entirely driven by AI. First, AI Mode and AI Overviews are new ad surfaces that expand Google's inventory. Second, AI capabilities are helping Google monetize queries that weren't monetized previously. Third, AI capabilities are improving ad targeting and, therefore, price per click.

I expect Google Search to deliver a 15%+ growth year in the coming years.

Google Cloud

Google Cloud revenues represent 15% of Alphabet sales, growing the fastest of all lines. Cloud has 14 product lines with $1b+ ARRs; it exited with "New Customer Velocity" double the rate of Q1, and $1b+ deals in 2025 were higher than the previous three years combined.

In Q4 alone, Google Cloud signed an astounding $100 billion gross new backlog. With this level of momentum, I expect Google Cloud to roughly maintain the 48% growth rate it delivered in Q4, reflecting a meaningful acceleration from the 36% growth in 2025.

YouTube, Subscription & Devices

Representing 22% of sales, I expect to see meaningful acceleration in these two lines, especially YouTube ads. Google has been pushing YouTube Premium very aggressively, which has been weighing down on YouTube Ads growth (and which is why the two revenue lines should be judged together).

Starting in 2026, I expect to see more balanced growth between the two lines, especially in the second half, where the midterm elections should be a major tailwind for ads (vs. a headwind in 2025).

Beyond that, Google's entertainment businesses continue to consistently gain market share. With the help of AI-driven improvements to content creation, recommendation systems, and ad targeting, I expect 15%+ growth here for years to come.

Nearing FCF Inflection

It's not just growth, though. Alphabet is the best positioned to deliver AI-led accelerated growth while maintaining top-tier margins, resulting in outsized returns.

This is perhaps the most popular talking point in the market right now: ROI on AI capital spending.

The truth is, for anybody willing to do some digging, the ROI question has already been answered. In fact, on a capex-to-orders basis, Google Cloud's ROI has never been higher, dropping to a record 27% in Q4. In other words, for each $1 of capex, Google Cloud signed roughly $4 of commitments.

Now, it's true that capital intensity is expected to reach unprecedented levels, with the $180 billion capex guidance representing ~38% of sales. However, as the previous graph showed, this capex is generating record future revenues. Meanwhile, operating cash flow margins are also reaching all-time highs.

As long as we're in this hyper-growth stage, with demand far outstripping supply, capital intensity will remain high, but it will ease eventually. Meanwhile, the operating cash flow margin is here to stay.

I wish I knew when exactly that tipping point is going to occur, but what I do know is that when it does, it will be too late to jump on the train.

Two Extraordinary Options Investors Are Getting For Free - Gemini & Enterprise

Alphabet is clearly firing on all cylinders, and we've discussed the primary drivers for near-term performance. However, there are two lines of business that I view as untapped upside opportunities that Alphabet investors are getting for free. And, no, it's not Waymo, nor the potential for a huge standalone chip business if they decide to go that route.

The First "Option" - Gemini

Google's Gemini traffic share grew 5x over the past year, with MAU count surpassing 750 million. The vast majority of this engagement is completely unmonetized, as Google presumably awaits OpenAI (OPENAI) to incorporate ads in a meaningful way, hoping it will send more users towards an ad-free Gemini.

At some point, it's inevitable that Google, too, will start monetizing this surface. The list of companies that are better positioned than Google to monetize such a service is very short.

The Second "Option" - Enterprise AI

This option is more uncertain, but it's quite significant. Gemini consistently ranks among the top LLM models in terms of raw capabilities, yet Google has yet to tap the productivity market in a meaningful way.

Gemini doesn't really have its own rival to OpenAI's Codex and Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude Code, which are considered the leading enterprise-ready tools. Further, Gemini hasn't cracked the AI coding space yet.

From first principles, I don't see why Google's DeepMind won't be able to deliver on these fronts. Once they do, Google's usual competitive advantages should come into play, including a lower cost to serve, extraordinary latency, trustworthiness among enterprises, and best-in-class security.

If and when Google executes on this opportunity, it could be another huge revenue driver.

Valuation

At $295 per share, Google trades at 25 times forward earnings and 22 times next year's estimates.

Google is no longer the cheapest big tech company in terms of P/E, with both Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT) trading below, while Apple (AAPL) and Amazon (AMZN) are trading above.

In my mind, this is the right spot that reflects Google's moat vs. the others. With its TPUs, the DeepMind team, and Gemini being at the frontier, Google deserves a higher multiple vs. Microsoft or Meta, which are behind. Amazon's physical moat remains significant, and Apple's valuation is a topic for another article.

Conclusion

I estimate that at a minimum, Google will maintain its current multiple, which would result in 16%+ annual returns, in line with the expected growth.

That said, as I expect consistent beat & raises, multiple expansion is highly probable, and once the FCF inflection occurs, it's almost certain.

As such, I see potential for annual returns closer to 20%, justifying a reiteration of a Strong Buy rating.
